speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number nine. I'd like to ask you a little bit about DP camps and any experience you might have had.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [SIGHS] This is a very brief, strange story. The war was over with. We were back to flying again. I had, pretty much, decided that what I had seen I couldn't carry with me. I had to just put it aside and go on with my life. [SIGHS] I had a lot of points to come home. I could have come home before the green plan, is what they called it. If you had 121 points accumulated by battles, whatever, whatever, whatever, you could fly home, uh, on an old World War II bomber. And I-- and I went down with my before bag and my, uh that's two duffel bags-- to Frankfurt, and saw these B-24s and B-17s sitting out there with oil all over them. And I said, I ain't gonna do that. I'd been-- I pushed it close enough, 67 missions.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I'm not-- I'm going to-- I'm going to wait-- I could have gone home in July or August, as opposed to December, if I had wanted to fly home. I have enough points, but I wasn't going to get on one of those old, beat up airplanes. And they would have flown across the Atlantic Ocean, like I told you. Yeah. And-- and they lost, not a lot, but they lost half a dozen, or a dozen. You know, full of guys. I wasn't going to take a chance, so I waited and caught a boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, in the meantime, we were assigned-- we went-- we-- uh, we moved to, uh, uh, Badenheim, which was where President Roosevelt used to go when he was a boy, when he had polio, to take the mineral baths. Beautiful town. Beautiful town. And we were just outside of town in an air strip. And we-- we were targets for the new radar anti-aircraft guns. So what we-- all we did, we'd just fly a course back and forth, and changing altitudes from their command-- some commander, you know, pick up 1,000 feet, to see-- training these guys how to the use these radar guns. They would follow us. They-- once they got them locked onto us, no matter what we did, the gun would follow us, and lead us, and the whole thing. State of the art, after the war. And then we had a demonstration of fire power to-- our strip was located up on the high ground. And we took off, and we went right over the DP camp at about 500 feet. The DP camp was part of an old castle, I think, and-- because there was a huge building of some kind involved, and a multi-story building. And also, uh, it was all walled in and fenced in with barbed wire, just like the concentration camps. You couldn't get in, and you couldn't get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We took off over-- and there was slit trenches. I'm talking about hundreds of slit trenches. You know what a slit trench is? It's a trench where people used to go to the bathroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there-- and there was just a canvas, about this high, then little sticks with canvas awnings between the slit trenches. So you know, try to picture this. You and I have to go at the same time. And you're right there, and I'm right here. And that's it. And you can see me, and I can see you. It blew me away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were flying right over these. And they was going like this at us, and you know? We-- I mean, that's-- we weren't doing it on purpose, that was our pattern. Madder than-- they didn't like us a damn bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, they were bringing in trucks and taking-- they were sorting out the DPs. There-- I don't know. There must have been 25 or 30,000, or more. And they-- and there was mostly tents, and as I said, this big, huge building of some kind. When I went on this tour, this-- this guy who was working with the Amer-- with the military government-- and he was a linguist-- his job, and a bunch of other guys, was to find out who you were, where you were born, and-- and what you wanted to do. And tell ya-- now, if you were Russian and didn't want to go back to Russia, they made some-- I guess there were some circumstances. This guy explained it all to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews did not want to go back to Poland, period. Anywhere, but Poland. Most of the Jews, if these were-- and they weren't all Jews, there were some gypsies in there too-- wanted to go to Palestine or to the United States. I guess that's because they felt safest. I don't know. But that was the chosen. Where you want to go? Instead of saying back  to Poland, or back to Germany, or whatever, they wanted to go to Palestine or to the United States. So what did you observe when you went there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went in, the-- I-- I told you about the only reason I went in was because this guy says you gotta see this, you know, to see what I have to go through every day. And we had armed guards. I'm talking about the soldiers. These were not combat guys, because these-- these were new guys from the States who'd never seen any combat-- but they were trained. And they were armed with rifles, and machine guns, and bayonets. And there were a bunch of them. There was he and I and probably 20 soldiers that went-- took us in the camps. And-- and the people didn't do anything, except stare-- uh, uh, glare at you. Nobody spit at us. He said, don't be surprised if someone spits at you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then later, we talked. I says, why are they like that? He says, well Jesus, how'd you like to be in that place? You know, some of those people have been in there since the damn war ended. They don't have enough food. They're getting fed, but you-- it's-- you know, the inmates were running the institution. And so the-- whoever was the most powerful ones was the ones that made the decisions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all these other people could do was try to sort out who goes where and put them on a truck or a train that's going there. And a lot of people would lie like hell. They didn't have any ID, for the most part. You couldn't tell who they were, uh, unless you were a good-skilled interrogator, you know, and, uh, uh, could trap them up some way. That, you know, they'd try to deny that they were from Hungary, or wherever that they didn't want to go, so they could go someplace else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was that experience. It was pitiful. When I-- he said-- uh, this guy told me, he said, you know, there's been three of these people in this camp shot by Americans for stealing sheep from the neighboring farms around here. I said, my god, are-- he said, we're feeding them as much as we can, but they're-- they're distributing this food. He said, where-- you know, we give them all-- we give them everything we've got. And, uh, the other thing about this-- most of these people had-- were wearing clothing made from pocket material. You know what that-- you know, what a man's pant's pockets? I don't know what they call that. But there was a factory in the town that made uniforms for the Germans and had this material, thousands and thousands of yards. So these DPs, before-- way before we got there-- had got into this factory and taken all the good material and made clothes out of it. How many people do you reckon were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: God-- thousands. Thousands. Once in awhile, you'd see a gang of them out, like, uh, men, especially. And this guy that told me about the shootings, that these three, they were leaders that were leading, uh, raiding parties of men on-- uh, to get some sheep to-- to cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go inside at all? Inside the building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Went inside. Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the arrangements look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was the smell. There was no smell of any death, but, uh, those, uh, slit trenches--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did people sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, in tents. And, uh, and in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: On bunks? On the floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the-- yeah, they had tents and bed-- uh, bunks, GI bunks, you know, the canvas bunks. And, uh, they had medics. They had-- they had everything-- they had everything they could-- that they needed to do. The biggest problem was trying to get-- or trying to get these people to cooperate, so that you could get a-- get it all sorted out. And if it's-- The-- the poor guys that had the job of doing this just simply couldn't do it, because the people wouldn't cooperate. And they'd tell them, don't go outside of the-- of the camp. And evidently-- I never saw this, particularly. I mean, I never saw anybody get shot. I saw a body after, maybe a couple of days after. They left him laying out there in his white outfit. And evidently, they had to go out-- they would go out and raid the neighborhood farms for food, for sheep, chickens, whatever. Now, you tell me. I don't understand it. And To this day, I don't understand it. But there's a lot of stuff about this I don't understand. But that was my experience with DP camps. And-- and I felt [SIGHS] tremendous guilt that here-- that-- of all-- of-- of all the things that I had already seen and-- and-- and were perpetrated by these Nazis, these horrible Nazis, who I hated by now, and these SS guys, who I coulda killed, with not even given it a second thought, by then-- by then, you know, psychologically, I'm talking about. Here we were, in the fall of 1945, still trying to help these people that were victims of this whole damn thing, and we couldn't help them. We couldn't-- we didn't know what to do. Now, that's just one DP camp. I've heard Wiesenthal and also Elie Wiesel discuss the DP camps, personally, here at the University of Oregon. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just interested to know what you observed yourself, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what you observed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's it. With the-- the pathetic circumstances of the people who were in the camp, the fact is they all had clothing that were made from pocket material for German uniforms. The way-- the [INAUDIBLE], the, uh-- the inability of the Americans to organize it, at the time I was there-- they probably got it done, uh, at some point in time-- to have any kind of system, or control, or organization inside the-- whoever was the-- this was the-- this was power that was beyond our power to control. Whoever was-- they were dealing with in there, inside, had more power than we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to, um, go on and ask you about your post-war experiences--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --which were, uh, quite vast after that. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean, after the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I--

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you discharged?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got discharged December the 10th, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your rank at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a Tech Sergeant. I had been promoted. I was working my way back up, which I eventually did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, what did you do after you got out of the service?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my first thing I did was take about-- uh, well, the first thing I did, like a damn fool, was get a job with the post office. I don't know why I did that. I had $6,000 or $7,000 in cash in the bank.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tuscon. But I got a job with the post office. One of my buddy's got a job as-- uh, around Christmas time running parcel post trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so I got the job. It was a real stupid thing. I didn't-- just-- Christmas was over. I quit. And then, I went back to school. Then, I didn't-- I couldn't hack it. I told-- I-- I couldn't-- I couldn't hack it. I had the GI bill and all. I couldn't hack it in pre-med. Too hard for me. I wasn't that interested. I quit. I went-- I went to work for Sears and Roebuck on their training program, managerial training program, which was subsidized by the government. Ended up with the FW Woolworth Company in the same capacity. Stayed 10 years, became a store manager. Had my baby, one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we forgot that you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Evelyn Helen Glover. [POINTS] That little lady in there. And there's some more pictures over here. She was 15-years-old when we got married in July. She turned 16 in September. I was 17 and turned 18-- no, I had just turned 18, June the 30th. We got married July the 21st.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years has it been?

speaker: interviewee
text: It'll be 55 years this July. And we didn't tell anybody. I wasn't supposed to be married, uh, in the cadets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no. We didn't have any children. My daughter was born in March of '45. So she was-- she was, uh, born while I was in France, March of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three grandchildren, a boy, 32, who just became a father, which makes me a great-grandfather. The baby's name is, uh, Mason Charles Rivers. He's named after me. And if I call him Charlie, I'm going to be severely punished by my grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, after the war, share your experiences with your family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or with anyone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to-- I tried to talk to my brother, who had been in the-- in the-- in the Navy in the South Pacific. He thought it was bullshit, is what he told me. We were both about half in the bag from-- we had six or seven beers, or something like that. And he was telling me war experiences, I was telling him war experiences. I mentioned that I had been in these Nazi concentration camps, which he had never heard of. And when I was telling him about some of the cruelties I've seen, and the number of dead people, and so on and so forth, he just said, that's just bullshit. And we almost got into a fistfight, but we-- we stopped talking about it. And-- and I never mentioned that to anybody, until I met Bill [? Sarnoff ?], who is a local Jewish gentleman, first class, good pal of mine, good drinking buddy, uh, a PhD, the whole enchilada. And Bill got me involved with the Anne Frank Society. Bill was an interpreter at Buchenwald. He was in the US Navy and Solarno. When they liberated the camps, they needed interpreters. Bill speaks four or five languages, including Yiddish. They shipped him to Buchenwald, and he was there six weeks. And he had a-- a job that I couldn't-- I don't think I coulda done, which was interviewing each and every one of the-- not only Bill, but others like him-- interviewing each and every one of these inmates who could, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you get involved? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I bed your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you become involved with talking about your story? 1992. Bill [? Sarnoff ?] wrote, uh, uh, an article in the paper-- I didn't know him, uh, or even know of him-- and it was about Buchenwald. And I called him and said, you know, my name's Chuck Ferree, and I was at Buchenwald. And so we met for lunch. And-- and, I mean, here's a guy that I could relate to, you know, and knew what I was talking about. Now, where do you live now? Because you said locally, where do you live today?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Eugene, Oregon. But this-- yeah, this is where Bill-- Bill lives. Bill is from California, also. I spent, uh-- I spent from 1947 until 1986 in California with the, uh-- I never did finish that part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can I ask you, what prompted you to give your testimony today?

speaker: interviewee
text: What prompted me to give my testimony was, number one, I-- I've realized that it's just a matter of time until there won't be any live people who can give live testimony. There won't be any live, uh, victims, survivors, witnesses, [CLEARS THROAT] liberators. It's all going to be in books and on tapes. And we're all going to be gone, and-- and we need as much powerful documentation as we can to overcome the attempts on the part of the revisionists to tell us that the Holocaust never happened at all, or that they didn't kill six million Jews and 10 or 15 million other people, that they never gassed any people on German soil, that they never shot little babies in the head in their mother's arms and blew their brains all over their mothers. All these things happened. I-- I never witnessed any of this as a combat soldier, but I know that it happened, because I've been talking to people since 1992 who saw this happened, or who lost families this way in-- in Germany, and Poland, and Czechoslovakia, in Russia, Austria, Hungary, you name it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to add to your testimony that you might have not given us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether that statement that I wrote out at-- for one of these, uh, panels-- I think you have it. I don't-- I don't know where it-- OK. Oh, here it is. Pardon me, while I read a little statement that I've used at-- at some of the Anne Frank, uh, panel discussions. 'Simon Wiesenthal, a victim of the Holocaust--' who I've have had the pleasure of meeting on two separate occasions-- 'and who's devoted his life since the end of World War II trying to bring Nazi war criminals to justice is quoted as saying, 'An airplane crash which takes the lives of 150 people is called a catastrophe, while the murder of over 6 million Jews by the Nazis is simply a statistics. The crimes committed and the crimes planned by the Nazis are so unspeakably monstrous that the human mind fails to comprehend their full dimensions. What happened is now known by most of the world. The question of why it happened, remains unanswered. And in spite of all the documented evidence, official statements from every government, German government over the years, as well as many other governmental officials involved with the Holocaust, the evidence of the Nuremberg Trials, and many other war crime trials, plus thousands of eyewitnesses, survivors, friends of survivors, relatives of survivors, rescuers, liberators, childrens of liberators, resistance fighters, and a host of others who know what happened, who saw it happen, had it happen to them, and the world still has to listen to, so-called, experts who try to tell us that it never happened at all. Personally, I know it's all true. I'll never forget my tiny part in World War II. And I pray no one ever says to me that it didn't happen.''

speaker: interviewer
text: We'd like to thank you, very much, on behalf of the Foundation, for sharing your very important testimony with us. Thank you, very much, Mr. Ferree.

speaker: interviewee
text: Been a pleasure, for me. And-- and I feel like a tremendous load has been taken off of my shoulders by you folks and by this program. And I'm privileged to be a part of it. Honored. That is my grandfather, [?Teofilo?] Contraris. Uh, picture was taken in Prescott, Arizona, Miller Valley. Those are his brothers and his father on the far right. I don't have an idea what his name is. He was from Spain. The picture was taken about 1915, around in there, I'm guessing. I'd say I'm guessing. My mother was born in 19-- my mother was born in 1902. This could have been taken around that time, around the turn of the century. That's my best guess. The woman on the right, the olderly-- the, uh, olderly-- elderly lady, is my-- my mother's grandmother. Uh, I have no-- I don't know hardly anything about that part of the family. Part of them were Mexican. And you can see Indian features. They were Arapaho Indians, uh, mixed with Spanish and Mexicans. They migrated from Mexico before the turn of the century, and crossed the Colorado River on rafts, and opened a-- a-- founded a ranch in Miller Valley, Arizona, near Prescott. Amazing. My paternal grandfather, John McQuarter Ferree. This picture had to be taken 20 years, maybe 30 years before the turn of the century, which would have been 1860, 1870, around in there. My dad was born in 1900, so this-- this is my grandfather. His father, I would guess his age to be in the late 60s, early 70s. First time I've ever noticed the similarity between my dad and his dad. This is-- this is my mother, Charlotte Contraris Ferree. Her middle name was Francis. She was born in Prescott, Arizona in 1902. She lived to be 86 years old. She had another child, my brother, Ben, Benjamin Francis, who passed away 15 years ago. A-- a wonderful woman, a barrel of laughs, fun to be with, and a good mother. This picture is one of the first, uh, vacations of the family that I recall with my mother and father. My father, of course, is the adult. Uh, the little, dark headed kid is my little brother, Benny. And the other guy on the right is me at the age of about seven, which means the picture would have been taken about 192-- about 1930, at Picacho Lake, which is about 60 miles from Tucson, in the desert. We went fishing for catfish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother took the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother took the picture. And we slept in the back of that International truck. This picture was taken in Long Beach, California. The big, tall, husky, good-looking guy on the left is my brother, Benny, who unfortunately has left us. This is my bride of 54 years, Evelyn. I call her Dickie. Her brother named her Dickie, because he wanted a little brother-- D-I-C-K-I-E. This is me, uh, at the age-- this was taken at Long Beach, California, about 1949 or '50. This is my father and my mother. My father was in the VA hospital in Long Beach, and we were visiting. --August of 1945 in Frankfurt, Germany. I was 21 years old-- had just turned 21 years old. Stinson L-5. This is a photograph of a-- of a, uh, Stinson L-5 liaison airplane, the kind of plane that we flew in and out of the concentration camps in 1945. It's a two-seater plane, 265 horsepower engine. The passenger were officers from SHAEF, General Eisenhower's headquarters, who were going into the various camps to assess, uh, the situation and advise the General what should be done by the American forces. This picture is of my daughter, Sandy, and her husband, Jack. The last name is Wigmore. We are in San Francisco, about to board the boat that would take us to Alcatraz Island. My daughter is current--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year? When?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- uh, I think the picture was taken in 1991. This is my oldest grandson, Dane Legrand Rivers, who is now 32 years old. At the time this picture was taken, he was a junior at the University of Oregon. He, of course, has since graduated. He is a new father, December the 29th, 1996, which makes me a great-grandfather on that same day. This is my granddaughter, Lisa Marie Rivers. The picture was taken in 1986, or '87, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Eugene, Oregon. [INAUDIBLE] grandson, Joshua Rivers, who is-- uh, the picture was taken in 1986.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Eugene?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Eugene, Oregon. The bride and groom, Dane and Sara Rivers, taken In Bend, Oregon on the day of their wedding, May the 19th, 1992. --addition into our family. This is Mason Charles Rivers on the-- about at one half hour after his birth, December the 29th, 1996. He is now approaching his second month on Earth, and he looks quite different now than he does in this picture. But he's a beautiful baby, and we love him dearly.
